intitule: Lesson 6 Assessment: Ability & Permission (can, could, may)

enonce.texte: Read each sentence carefully. Choose the correct modal auxiliary (can, could, may, can't, mustn't) based on the context of ability, permission, or prohibition.

questions.texte: Identify the correct modal to complete the sentence: "When I was six years old, I ______ swim very well."

sousQuestions.texte: Justify your choice by explaining the grammatical rule.
sousQuestions.reponse: We use 'could' to express a general ability in the past (past of 'can').

questions.texte: Choose the correct option: "Excuse me, ______ I borrow your pen, please?" (Formal permission)

sousQuestions.texte: Why is 'may' more appropriate here than 'can'?
sousQuestions.reponse: 'May' is more formal and polite than 'can' when asking for permission.

questions.texte: Select the sentence that expresses a strong prohibition:
questions.reponse: You mustn't smoke in the hospital.

sousQuestions.texte: Explain the difference between 'mustn't' and 'can't' in terms of prohibition.
sousQuestions.reponse: 'Mustn't' expresses a strong prohibition based on rules or law (it is forbidden), while 'can't' expresses a lack of ability or informal permission denial (not allowed).

intitule: Real-Life Problem Solving: School and Public Rules

enonce.texte: You are the head of the disciplinary committee at your high school in Yaoundé. Read the following situations and formulate the appropriate rules using can, cannot (can't), may, mustn't, or could.

questions.texte: During the national exam (Probatoire), a student asks to go to the toilet. What do you say to give formal permission?
questions.reponse: You may go to the toilet.

sousQuestions.texte: Rewrite the rule as a prohibition: students are forbidden to talk during the exam.
sousQuestions.reponse: You mustn't talk during the exam.

questions.texte: A student with a broken arm asks if they can use a computer for the essay writing part. You allow it. What do you say?

sousQuestions.texte: If the same student asks about their past ability (last term) to write fast, which modal do you use?
sousQuestions.reponse: You could write fast last term.

questions.texte: At the school library, a student is playing loud music. You must tell them it is forbidden. Formulate the rule.
questions.reponse: You mustn't play loud music in the library.

sousQuestions.texte: Now, give a general statement of ability: The library has new computers, so students are now able to access the internet. (Use 'can')
sousQuestions.reponse: Students can access the internet in the library now.

enonce.texte: Analyze the following scenarios related to Health (Lesson 30-31) and Civic responsibilities (Lesson 27). Fill in the blanks with the correct modal and justify your answer based on the context.

questions.texte:
Context: A doctor at the Central Hospital in Douala is speaking to a patient who has malaria.

"You ______ take this medicine twice a day. It is an obligation for your recovery."

sousQuestions.texte: Why is 'have to' also possible, but 'must' is stronger here?
sousQuestions.reponse: 'Must' expresses an internal obligation or strong advice from the speaker (doctor's authority), while 'have to' often expresses an external rule. Here, the doctor is giving a direct order for health.

questions.texte:
Context: A journalist is reporting on gender equality (Lesson 28).

"In Cameroon, women ______ now vote and run for office. This shows progress in democracy."

sousQuestions.texte: Transform this sentence into a past ability sentence about the year 1990 (if the law changed later).
sousQuestions.reponse: In 1990, women could not vote, but today they can.

questions.texte:
Context: A police officer at a polling station (Lesson 27) sees someone trying to vote twice.

"You ______ vote twice! This is against the law."

sousQuestions.texte: What is the difference between 'mustn't vote twice' and 'can't vote twice' in this legal context?
sousQuestions.reponse: 'Mustn't' indicates a moral/legal prohibition (it is forbidden). 'Can't' would simply mean 'not able to', which is not the intended meaning here. The law explicitly forbids it.

enonce.texte:
Read the text below and answer the questions using modals of ability, permission, and advice.

Text: 'In many villages in the North Region, teenage girls who become pregnant often drop out of school. They cannot continue their education because of shame or lack of support. However, a new NGO is helping these young mothers. Now, they can attend special classes in the evening. The government says schools must not (mustn't) exclude pregnant girls anymore. The girls may also join vocational training centers to learn a trade. Last year, without this help, they could only stay at home. Today, with the right support, they can build a better future.'

questions.texte: According to the text, what was the general situation for pregnant teenagers in the past regarding school?
questions.reponse: They could not continue their education / They could only stay at home.

sousQuestions.texte: Quote the sentence that shows past lack of ability.
sousQuestions.reponse: 'Last year, without this help, they could only stay at home.'

questions.texte: What permission does the government give to schools regarding pregnant girls?
questions.reponse: The government says schools must not (mustn't) exclude pregnant girls.

sousQuestions.texte: Rewrite this as a positive permission using 'may'.
sousQuestions.reponse: Pregnant girls may attend school like any other student.

questions.texte: Based on the text, what ability do the teenage mothers have now that they didn't have before?
questions.reponse: They can attend special classes in the evening and join vocational training centers.
